---
title: "Model Interpretation"
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path("..").resolve()
ranking  = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "data_revealed/04_tables/county_attractiveness_ranking.csv", index_col="rank")
shap_df  = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "data_revealed/04_tables/shap_values.csv").set_index("county_key")
prep     = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "data_revealed/04_tables/county_preprocessed.csv")

s1_cols  = [c for c in shap_df.columns if c.startswith("s1_")]
s2_cols  = [c for c in shap_df.columns if c.startswith("s2_")]
s1_feats = [c[3:] for c in s1_cols]
s2_feats = [c[3:] for c in s2_cols]

sv1 = shap_df[s1_cols].values
sv2 = shap_df[s2_cols].values
```

## SHAP Framework

We use **SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)** with `TreeExplainer` for exact SHAP
values — not approximations. SHAP assigns each feature a contribution to the
model's output for each individual county, with the sum of contributions equaling
the difference between the county's prediction and the global mean prediction.

$$
f(x) = \phi_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j
$$

where $\phi_0$ is the base value (mean prediction) and $\phi_j$ is feature $j$'s
SHAP contribution. Positive SHAP → pushes prediction up; negative SHAP → pushes down.

All 3,138 county SHAP values were pre-computed and stored for efficient lookup.

## Global Feature Importance — Stage 1 (Presence)

Mean |SHAP| across all counties ranks features by their average contribution to
the presence probability. This is the model's answer to: *"What structural factors
most determine whether a county attracts any data center?"*

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Stage 1 mean |SHAP| — feature importance for P(has DC). Top 20 features shown."
mean_abs_s1 = np.abs(sv1).mean(axis=0)
imp_s1 = pd.DataFrame({"feature": s1_feats, "mean_abs_shap": mean_abs_s1})
imp_s1 = imp_s1.sort_values("mean_abs_shap", ascending=True).tail(20)

fig = px.bar(
    imp_s1, x="mean_abs_shap", y="feature", orientation="h",
    labels={"mean_abs_shap": "Mean |SHAP|", "feature": ""},
    color="mean_abs_shap", color_continuous_scale="Blues",
)
fig.update_layout(
    coloraxis_showscale=False, plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=10), height=520,
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee"),
)
fig.show()
```

## Global Feature Importance — Stage 2 (Scale)

Among counties that already have data centers, what drives the count? Stage 2 answers
this on the positive subset, revealing a different hierarchy.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Stage 2 mean |SHAP| — feature importance for E[count | DC present]. Top 20 features shown."
mean_abs_s2 = np.abs(sv2).mean(axis=0)
imp_s2 = pd.DataFrame({"feature": s2_feats, "mean_abs_shap": mean_abs_s2})
imp_s2 = imp_s2.sort_values("mean_abs_shap", ascending=True).tail(20)

fig = px.bar(
    imp_s2, x="mean_abs_shap", y="feature", orientation="h",
    labels={"mean_abs_shap": "Mean |SHAP|", "feature": ""},
    color="mean_abs_shap", color_continuous_scale="Purples",
)
fig.update_layout(
    coloraxis_showscale=False, plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=10), height=520,
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee"),
)
fig.show()
```

## Stage 1 vs Stage 2 — Rank Shift Analysis

The two stages ask fundamentally different questions. Comparing feature ranks between
them reveals which factors drive *entry* vs *clustering*.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Feature rank in Stage 1 vs Stage 2 (by mean |SHAP|). Lines show rank movement between stages."
rank_s1 = pd.Series(np.abs(sv1).mean(axis=0), index=s1_feats).rank(ascending=False).astype(int)
rank_s2 = pd.Series(np.abs(sv2).mean(axis=0), index=s2_feats).rank(ascending=False).astype(int)

top_feats = rank_s1.nsmallest(15).index.tolist()
rank_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": top_feats,
    "Stage 1 Rank": rank_s1[top_feats].values,
    "Stage 2 Rank": rank_s2[top_feats].values,
})
rank_df["shift"] = rank_df["Stage 1 Rank"] - rank_df["Stage 2 Rank"]
rank_df["color"] = rank_df["shift"].apply(lambda x: "#43a047" if x < -3 else ("#ef5350" if x > 3 else "#90a4ae"))
rank_df = rank_df.sort_values("Stage 1 Rank")

fig = go.Figure()
for _, row in rank_df.iterrows():
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[1, 2], y=[row["Stage 1 Rank"], row["Stage 2 Rank"]],
        mode="lines+markers+text",
        line=dict(color=row["color"], width=2),
        marker=dict(size=8, color=row["color"]),
        text=["", row["feature"]],
        textposition="middle right",
        showlegend=False,
        hovertemplate=f"{row['feature']}<br>S1 rank: {row['Stage 1 Rank']}<br>S2 rank: {row['Stage 2 Rank']}<extra></extra>",
    ))
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(tickvals=[1, 2], ticktext=["Stage 1<br>(Presence)", "Stage 2<br>(Scale)"],
               showgrid=False, range=[0.8, 2.8]),
    yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed", showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee",
               title="Rank (1 = most important)"),
    plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    margin=dict(l=10, r=180, t=20, b=20), height=480,
)
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
rank_df_display = rank_df[["feature", "Stage 1 Rank", "Stage 2 Rank", "shift"]].copy()
rank_df_display.columns = ["Feature", "Stage 1 Rank", "Stage 2 Rank", "Rank Change (↑ = rises in S2)"]
rank_df_display = rank_df_display.sort_values("Stage 1 Rank").set_index("Feature")
rank_df_display
```

## Key Interpretive Findings

### 1. Power Infrastructure as the Entry Gate

`clean_energy_jobs` (rank 1, S1) and `grid_infrastructure_jobs` (rank 2, S1) are the
two strongest presence predictors. These are not directly measuring power *availability*
but rather the depth of the **power sector workforce** — a proxy for grid capacity,
interconnection maturity, and the density of utility infrastructure needed to serve a
hyperscale campus.

This aligns with industry reporting: power access (not just price) is consistently
cited as the top constraint on new data center development.

### 2. The Agglomeration Signal in `wage_information`

`wage_information` — the average wage in information-sector occupations — rises from
**rank 4 in Stage 1 to rank 1 in Stage 2**. This is the clearest agglomeration signal
in the model:

- At the presence stage, IT labor is important but secondary to power infrastructure
- At the scale stage, it becomes the dominant factor — established markets attract
  more facilities because they've already built deep IT talent pools

This is consistent with the "tech cluster" dynamic: once a few large operators anchor
in a metro, the labor market deepens, which attracts more operators, reinforcing the cluster.

### 3. Geographic Confounding in Hazard Risk Indices

NRI hazard risk indices (lightning, hurricane, tornado, earthquake) appear in the
**top 8 Stage 1 predictors** but fall to **ranks 20–30 in Stage 2**. This pattern
is diagnostic of **geographic confounding**:

The existing US data center market is concentrated in Virginia (high lightning risk),
Texas (hurricane/tornado risk), and Georgia (coastal storm risk). The model learns
this geographic correlation in Stage 1. But once we condition on positive counties
in Stage 2, the hazard indices lose predictive power — confirming they are proxies
for *where the market already is*, not genuine attractors.

::: {.callout-important}
**Policy implication**: Hazard risk should not be used as a site selection criterion
based on this model. Counties with lower hazard risk are not inherently less attractive —
the observed correlation reflects historical accident, not structural causation.
:::

### 4. Electricity Cost Sensitivity at Scale

`commercial_price` rises from rank 9 (S1) to rank 4 (S2). Cost sensitivity is
a **scale-dependent phenomenon**: small operators may accept above-market power costs
if other factors (labor, land, connectivity) are favorable, but hyperscale campuses
consuming 100s of MW are acutely sensitive to cents-per-kWh differences.

## County Deep Dive — Top 3 Profiles

```{python}
#| echo: false
def county_profile(county_key, label):
    row = ranking[ranking["county_key"] == county_key].iloc[0]
    sv1_row = shap_df.loc[county_key, s1_cols].values.astype(float)
    sv2_row = shap_df.loc[county_key, s2_cols].values.astype(float)

    idx1 = np.argsort(np.abs(sv1_row))[-8:][::-1]
    idx2 = np.argsort(np.abs(sv2_row))[-8:][::-1]

    profile = pd.DataFrame({
        "Stage 1 Feature": [s1_feats[i] for i in idx1],
        "S1 SHAP": [sv1_row[i] for i in idx1],
        "Stage 2 Feature": [s2_feats[i] for i in idx2],
        "S2 SHAP": [sv2_row[i] for i in idx2],
    })
    return row, profile

for ck, lbl in [
    ("Virginia||Loudoun County",   "#1 Loudoun County, VA"),
    ("Texas||Travis County",       "#4 Travis County, TX"),
    ("North Carolina||New Hanover County", "#80 New Hanover County, NC (zero DC)"),
]:
    row, profile = county_profile(ck, lbl)
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"{lbl}")
    print(f"  DCs: {row['num_datacenters']:.1f}  |  P(presence): {row['p_presence']:.3f}  "
          f"|  Score: {row['attractiveness']:.3f}")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "SHAP attribution for Loudoun County, VA — the top-ranked county globally."
ck = "Virginia||Loudoun County"
sv1_row = shap_df.loc[ck, s1_cols].values.astype(float)
sv2_row = shap_df.loc[ck, s2_cols].values.astype(float)

fig = go.Figure()
for sv, feats, name, color_pos, color_neg in [
    (sv1_row, s1_feats, "Stage 1 — Presence", "#ef5350", "#42a5f5"),
    (sv2_row, s2_feats, "Stage 2 — Scale",    "#e53935", "#1565c0"),
]:
    idx = np.argsort(np.abs(sv))[-10:]
    y = [feats[i] for i in idx]
    x = [float(sv[i]) for i in idx]
    cols = [color_pos if v > 0 else color_neg for v in x]
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        name=name, x=x, y=y, orientation="h",
        marker_color=cols,
        hovertemplate="%{y}: %{x:.4f}<extra></extra>",
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode="overlay", plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=30, b=10), height=420,
    xaxis_title="SHAP value", legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02),
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee"),
)
fig.add_vline(x=0, line_width=1, line_color="#444")
fig.show()
```
